original: Macedonian army led by Philip II defeats the combined forces of Athens and Thebes in the Battle of Chaeronea, securing Macedonian hegemony over Greece and the Aegean Sea
Second Punic War: Battle of Cannae - Carthaginian army led by Hannibal defeats a numerically superior Roman army under the command of consuls Lucius Aemilius Paullus and Gaius Terentius Varro
St Stephen I's death ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Frederick of Lorraine elected as the first Belgian Pope Stephen IX [X]
King William II of England (William Rufus) is killed by an arrow shot by Sir Walter Tyrell while hunting in the New Forest
Renaissance humanist and physician Hieronymus Münzer sets off from Nuremburg on a journey through Europe to the Iberian Peninsula, fleeing the plague
French troops leave Flanders
Battle of Rijmenam: Spanish Habsburg forces defeated by Dutch troops (Eighty Years' War)
Leiden University names Rudolph Snellius as math professor
Spanish garrison of Meurs surrenders to an Anglo-Dutch army led by Maurice of Nassau and Robert Bertie
Battle of Vågen: English warships attack a Dutch merchant and treasure fleet in neutral Bergen, Norway, but the English are forced to retreat after suffering 421 dead and wounded
French expedition against Barbarians in Tunis and Algiers
Daniel Quare receives a British patent for his portable barometer
Austria joins Triple Alliance of Britain, France and the Dutch Republic forming the Quadruple Alliance, to fight against Spain
Swiss alpine explorer Horace Bénédict de Saussure becomes the third person to reach the top of Mont Blanc
First US census conducted; population is 3,929,214, including 697,624 slaves
Samuel Briggs and his son granted US patent for a nail-making machine
First parachute jump in US by Frenchman Louis-Charles Guille over Jersey City
Ten-Day Campaign begins; Dutch army occupies Belgium
Battle of Bad Axe, Wisconsin: 1,300 Illinois militia defeat Sauk and Fox Native Americans, ending the Black Hawk War
First mailboxes installed in Boston and New York City streets
The Government of India is transferred from the East India Company to the British Crown
Skirmish at Dug Springs, Missouri results in Union victory
Saratoga Racecourse, America's oldest thoroughbred racetrack, opens its inaugural meet with four days of racing
Trans Atlantic Cable being laid by SS Great Eastern between Great Britain and America snaps and is lost
Tower Subway, the world's first underground tube railway, opens in London
First trial run of San Francisco cable car in Clay Street between Kearny and Jones
San Francisco Public Library opens with 5,000 volumes
British Parliament officially adopts Greenwich Mean Time (GMT)
Chester A. Hodge of Beloit, Wisconsin, patents "spur" barbed wire
George A. Wheeler is granted a US patent for a prototype of the escalator
Death duties are first introduced in Britain
Dutch Society for Women's Suffrage receives royal charter
Unsuccessful uprising of Macedonians against the Ottoman Empire
Chicago White Sox beat Boston Americans 3-0 to start an AL record 19-game MLB win streak
Army Air Corps formed as Army takes first delivery from Wright Brothers
President of Haiti Antoine Simon is ousted in a coup d'état by General Cincinnatus Leconte and flees Port-au-Prince aboard the Haitian warship 17 Décembre, bound for the United States
Belgian government receives German ultimatum
German press falsely reports that French have bombed Nuremberg
German troops overthrow Luxembourg
Germany and Turkey sign secret treaty of alliance
Great Britain mobilizes
WWI: Russian troops invade East Prussia
World War I: Austrian sabotage causes the sinking of the Italian battleship Leonardo da Vinci in Taranto
Japan announces that it is deploying troops to Siberia in the aftermath of World War I
After three hours of deliberation, a Chicago jury acquits eight Chicago White Sox players accused in the Black Sox scandal; the next day, they are banned from organized baseball for life
China is hit by a typhoon, killing about 60,000 people
A's first baseman Joe Hauser sets an AL record of 14 total bases in a game with three home runs and a double as Philadelphia beats the Cleveland Indians 12-4 at Dunn Field
Philadelphia first baseman Don Hurst sets an NL record with six consecutive games hitting a home run in the Phillies' 2-0 win against the Pittsburgh Pirates
Referendum in favor of autonomous status for Spanish Catalonia garners 99.5% of the vote; about 75% of eligible voters participate
First airplane train: airplane tows three mail gliders behind it
William Franks twirls an Indian club overhead 17,280 times in 1 hour
Americans Cornelius Johnson, Dave Albritton, and Delos Thurber sweep the high jump medals at the Berlin Olympics
Finnish trifecta in the 10,000 m final at the Berlin Olympics as Ilmari Salminen beats teammates Arvo Askola and Volmari Iso-Hollo
Marihuana Tax Act of 1937 passes in America, essentially rendering marijuana and all its by-products illegal
MLB conducts the first test of bright yellow baseballs during Brooklyn Dodgers vs. St. Louis Cardinals doubleheader
US Hatch Act prohibits political activity by federal workers
German 11th Army surrounds 20 Soviet divisions at Oeman
Jews are expelled from Hungarian Ruthenia
250 Dutch Catholic Jews are arrested and transported to Amersfoort camp
Colonel general Hermann Hoth's Panzer army reaches Kotelnikovo
Armed revolt by 800 prisoners at Treblinka Extermination Camp: crematorium destroyed; 200 escape the compound, but only 100 survive
Sunderland seaplanes sink U-706 and U-106
Jewish survivors of Kovono Ghetto emerge from their bunker
Turkey breaks diplomatic relationship with Nazi Germany
Danish swimmer Greta Andersen (1:06.3) touches out American Ann Curtis by 0.2 seconds in the women's 100 m freestyle at the London Olympics
Fanny Blankers-Koen of the Netherlands wins the 100 m in 11.9 at the London Olympics, her first of unprecedented three individual track and field gold medals
Italians Adolfo Consolini and Giuseppe Tosi go 1-2 in the men's discus final at the London Olympics
Hungarian boxer László Papp wins his second consecutive Olympic gold medal
KCPQ TV channel 13 in Tacoma-Seattle, WA (IND) begins broadcasting
Tahar Ben Ammar is appointed premier of Tunisia
USSR performs a nuclear test
Lovell Telescope's first light (first astronomical image) by what was then the world's largest steerable telescope at Jodrell Bank, England

American pilots Jim Heth and Bill Burkhart take off from Dallas, Texas, in "The Old Scotchman," their modified Cessna 172, in an effort to break the airplane flight endurance record; they fly for slightly over 50 days, eclipsing the existing record by more than 3 days
Jordan and Iraq dissolve their Arab Federation after 3 months
Milwaukee Braves outfielder Bill Bruton hits three triples in an 11-5 win against the St. Louis Cardinals; two of them with bases loaded, the first time in the 20th century

Baseball's new Continental League formally disbands without ever having played a game after established MLB promises of expansion, including New York City, achieve the owners' desired effect; on August 2, 1960, the Continental League formally disbands
Cyrille Adoula becomes Prime Minister of Congo
St. Louis Cardinals (NFL) beat Toronto Argonauts (CFL) 36-7 in an exhibition game in Toronto, Canada
The Beatles perform their first gig as the house band at Liverpool's Cavern Club
30th Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: All-Stars 20, Green Bay 17 (65,000 attendees)
Dutch government gives Indonesia export guarantees
North Vietnam fires at the US Navy destroyer USS Maddox in what becomes known as the Gulf of Tonkin Incident, eventually escalating US involvement in the Vietnam War
Race riot in Jersey City, New Jersey
Morley Safer sends first Vietnam report, indicating the US is losing
Radio Vila in the New Hebrides begins broadcasting
NFL New Orleans Saints play their first-ever preseason game, losing to the Los Angeles Rams 16-7 at Anaheim Stadium in California
NYC's recently established Landmarks Preservation Commission designates Grand Central Terminal train station as a landmark, thereby protecting its facade and interior architectural features
The second Blackwall Tunnel opens to traffic under the River Thames in Greenwich, London
US Lunar Orbiter 5 is launched and enters lunar orbit on August 5
35th Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: Green Bay 34, All-Stars 17 (69,917 attendees)

Ron Hansen (Washington Senators) and Tim Cullen (Chicago White Sox) become the first MLB players to be traded for each other twice in the same season; they had been traded in February in opposite directions
France performs a nuclear test at Fangataufa Island
Rubber bullets are used for the first time in Northern Ireland during "The Troubles"
Gold hits a record $70 an ounce in London
104°F (40°C) in Providence, Rhode Island (state record)
107°F (42°C) in Chester/New Bedford, Massachusetts (state record)
In his comeback Test, England cricket batsman Geoff Boycott is unbeaten on 80 after a first innings 107 as England beats Australia by seven wickets in the third Test at Trent Bridge
MLB New York Mets purchase contract of outfielder José Cardenal from Philadelphia Phillies between games of a double-header between the two teams
Washington, D.C. trial attorney Edward Bennett Williams buys MLB's Baltimore Orioles from Jerold Hoffberger for reported $12.3 million
Cuban super-heavyweight Teofilo Stevenson becomes the first fighter to win three Olympic gold medals in the same division, scoring a 4-1 points decision over Pyotr Zayev (Soviet Union) in Moscow
Fascist bomb attack on Bologna Centrale railway station in Italy kills 85 and injures 200
Roger Ebert's "Movie News" premieres on ABC FM network
STS-8 vehicle moves to launch pad
US District Court begins trial against Yonkers for alleged race discrimination
Jeff Blatnick becomes the first American to win a gold medal in Greco-Roman wrestling at the Los Angeles Olympics while in remission for Hodgkin’s disease
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Delta Air Lines Flight 191, operated by a Lockheed L-1011 TriStar, crashes on approach to Dallas Fort Worth Airport during a thunderstorm, killing 137 and injuring 25
Five die in a train crash in Westminster, Colorado
Lee Elder fires a record 11-under-par 61 on his way to winning the Senior PGA Merrill Lynch/Golf Digest Commemorative at Sleepy Hollow CC; the record stands for 11 years
PC born today
Cincinnati outfielder Eric Davis is seventh to hit 30 home runs and steal 30 bases in one season as he homers in Reds 5-4 win against the Giants
Don Brown sets a flight record for a handbow (1,336 yards, 1 foot, and 3 inches)
Michael Andretti runs the fastest Indy car race in history (171.49 mph) to win the Marlboro 500 at Michigan International Speedway in Brooklyn, Michigan
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Raymond Acevedo (16) retires from pop singing boy band Menudo
System Enhancement Association settles case with PKware (ARC vs. PKARC)
Iraq invades and occupies Kuwait, and the Emir flees to Saudi Arabia
Yankees rookie first baseman Kevin Maas hits his 10th MLB homer in a 6-5 loss against Detroit, becoming the fastest to reach that milestone in just 77 at-bats
Pan Am games open in Havana
Space Shuttle STS-43 (Atlantis 9) launches
American Jacki Joyner-Kersee becomes first athlete to win consecutive gold medals in the gruelling heptathlon with a 199 point win over Irina Belova (Soviet Union) at the Barcelona Olympics

Vitaly Scherbo of Belarus becomes the first gymnast to win six gold medals at one Olympics in Barcelona and the first to win four golds in one day's competition (pommel horse, vault, rings, and parallel bars)
Maryland investors, led by Baltimore attorney Peter Angelos, buy the MLB Baltimore Orioles team at auction in New York for $173 million, nearly $50 million more than ever paid for a baseball team
NYC radio (WFAN) personalityDon Imus' lung collapses
Shamrock Broadcasting, a Disney company, officially takes ownership of Cleveland's WMMS-FM 100.7 and WHK-AM 1420
Train crash in a tunnel in Vega de Anzo, Spain, kills 12
Explosion in lead/zinc mine in Guangxi, China, kills over 120
Noureddine Morceli runs a world record in the 3000 m (7:25.11)
Whitewater Congressional hearings begin
Saudi Arabia's King Fahd issues a decree replacing all members of the Council of Ministers who do not have blood ties to the royal Family
South African cricket batsman Graeme Smith blazes 259, and fast bowler Makhaya Ntini takes five wickets in each England innings as Proteas win the second Test by an innings and 92 runs at Lord's
MESSENGER spacecraft performs an Earth flyby

The largest trade in NBA history is completed as five teams combine to swap 13 players; the deal brings Antoine Walker and Jason Williams to Miami and leads to the Heat's first-ever championship that season
23 people are killed after two blasts in a fruit market in Lahore, Pakistan
American swimmerMichael Phelpswins an unprecedented third consecutive gold medal in the 200 m individual medley in 1:54.27 at the London Olympics
American swimmer Rebecca Soni becomes the first woman to win the 200 m breaststroke twice with a world record of 2:19.59 at the London Olympics
40 people are killed after an ammunition depot explodes in Homs, Syria
Naresh Sohal's orchestral work "The Cosmic Dance" first performed by the Royal Scottish National Orchestra, conducted by Peter Oundjian, at the Royal Albert Hall, London
Super Rugby Final, ANZ Stadium, Sydney: NSW Waratahs win their first SR title in a nail-biting 33-32 home effort against New Zealand's Canterbury Crusaders
First footage of white giraffes is posted by the Hirola Conservation Program in northeastern Kenya
First successful gene editing in human embryos to repair disease-causing mutation reported by scientists in "Nature"
More than a billion people around the world need glasses and 36 million are blind, according to a new study published in "The Lancet"
New cryptocurrency Bitcoin Cash is created via a hard fork of Bitcoin's blockchain technology and brand
Apple becomes the first American publicly listed company to reach $1 trillion in value
Fields Medal for mathematics is awarded to Caucher Birkar (his is stolen minutes later), Alessio Figalli, Peter Scholze, and Akshay Venkatesh
Japanese newspaper "Yomiuri Shimbun" reveals Tokyo Medical University is tampering with female entrance exams to ensure fewer than 30% are accepted
Oldest library in Germany confirmed unearthed in Cologne dating to 2 AD, possibly holding 20,000 scrolls
Pope Frances declares the death penalty unacceptable in all cases, reversing church teachings and adding to Catechism of the Roman Catholic Church
TikTok, the video-sharing social network, becomes available worldwide after merging with Musical.ly
Pedro Pierluisi is sworn in as the new Governor of Puerto Rico, replacing Ricardo A. Rosselló
Saudi Arabia announces new rules for women, including allowing them to travel independently abroad without a male guardian's permission
Seven-year-old boy undergoes surgery after 526 teeth are found inside his mouth in Chennai, India
Australian state of Victoria announces state of disaster and imposes further lockdown measures after spike in COVID-19 cases
Islamic State stages a jailbreak at a prison in the Afghan city of Jalalabad, placing bombs at its entrance, resulting in a 20-hour gunfight, 29 deaths, and over 300 prisoners at large
Mary Prince Day is established in Bermuda, recognizing Mary Prince, the first known Black woman to relate the story of her life, which helps hasten the abolition of slavery in the British Empire
South Africa confirms over 500,000 cases of COVID-19 with 10,107 deaths, highest total on the African continent
United Arab Emirates starts up the Arab world's first nuclear power station at the Barakah plant
Beijing records its heaviest rainfall in 140 years, registering 744.8 millimeters (29.3 inches) between July 29th and August 2nd as Typhoon Doksuri passes through

Fossils from the Ica Desert, Peru, may belong to the heaviest creature that ever lived, surpassing the blue whale. Named Perucetus colossus, its heavier bones suggest it could have weighed 85 to 340 tonnes
Australian Kaylee McKeown joins Roland Matthes of Germany (1968, 72) as the only swimmers to achieve the Olympic 100/200 m backstroke double when McKeown wins the 200m event in Paris
French swim sensation Léon Marchand wraps up his Paris Olympics individual program with gold in the 200 m individual medley, his fourth of the Games
In a rare podium sweep, France wins the men's BMX with Joris Daudet, Sylvain André, and Romain Mahieu going 1-2-3 at the Paris Olympics
SF Giants pitcher Blake Snell no-hits Cincinnati Reds 3-0 at Great American Ballpark, Cincinnati, Ohio
MLB Speedway Classic: regular season record crowd of 91,032 gathers for a baseball game at the Bristol Motor Speedway in Bristol, Tennessee, but game gets rained out in bottom of first inning
Treaty of Passau: EmperorCharles Vaccepts the Lutheran religion
English explorerHenry Hudsonenters the bay later named after him, the Hudson Bay
France offers Holy Roman EmperorCharles VImediation in his war against Turkey
Battle of the Nile: British Royal Navy under AdmiralHoratio Nelsonfurther decimates the French fleet
Napoleon Bonaparteis declared "Consul for Life" after winning a national referendum
Charles Xof France abdicates in favour of his grandson the Duc de Bordeaux
Lewis Carrollpublishes the children's novel "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland"
Legendary pitcherWalter Johnson, at 19, begins his 21-year Baseball Hall of Fame playing career with Washington with a 3-2 loss against Detroit
US issues the first Lincoln penny, featuring a portrait ofAbraham Lincolnon the front and wheat stalks on the reverse
Marcus Garveypresents his "Back to Africa" program in NYC
Benito Mussolinisigns peace treaty with Abyssinia (Ethiopia)
Carl David Andersondiscovers and photographs a positron, the first known antiparticle
Adolf Hitlerbecomes commander-in-chief of German armed forces
Albert Einsteinwrites to US PresidentFranklin D. Roosevelt, informing him of recent research on fission chain reactions, making possible the construction of "extremely powerful bombs"
Clermont-Ferrand sentences GeneralCharles de Gaulleto death in absentia
Gene Roddenberrypilots B-17 "Yankee Doodle," which crashes on takeoff, killing two
Lt.John F. Kennedy's PT boat 109 sinks in the Solomon Islands
Greek-American sopranoMaria Callasmakes her Italian debut in the title role of Amilcare Ponchielli's "La Gioconda" at the Verona Arena
17-year-old future world championFloyd Pattersonwins the gold medal in the middleweight division at the Helsinki Olympic Games with a first-round KO of Romanian Vasile Tita
Future Hall of Fame first basemanWillie McCoveyhits the first of his 521 MLB home runs in the SF Giants' 5-3 win against the Pittsburgh Pirates

The film "In the Heat of the Night," based on John Ball's novel of the same name, directed by Norman Jewison, and starringSidney PoitierandRod Steiger, premieres in New York and wins five Academy Awards in 1968, including Best Picture and Best Actor for Steiger
Bob Dylanmakes surprise appearance at his 10th high school reunion, in Hibbing, Minnesota
US PresidentRichard Nixonvisits Romania
"American Graffiti," directed byGeorge Lucasand starringRichard Dreyfussand Ron Howard, premieres at the Locarno International Film Festival in Switzerland
Future Baseball Hall of Fame third basemanGeorge Brettgets his first MLB hit on debut for the Kansas City Royals in a 3-1 win over the Chicago White Sox
Paul Volckeris confirmed by the U.S. Senate as Chairman of the Federal Reserve
Oakland A's outfielderRickey Hendersonsteals his 100th MLB base of the season in a 6-5 win against Seattle, becoming the first to steal 100 bases twice in the modern era
American athleteJackie Joyner-Kerseebeats her own world heptathlon record by 13 points (7,161) in Houston
Saddam Husseinoffers peace in open letter to Iran
US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bushorders troops to Saudi Arabia
Funk singerRick Jamesis arrested on charges of sexual assault and torture
Tom Seaver, Rollie Fingers, Hal Newhouser, and Bill McGowan are inducted into Baseball Hall of Fame
New York Supreme Court refusesHoward Stern's non-financial disclosure
The star-studded United States men's basketball team,Dream Team III, beat Yugoslavia 95-69 to win the gold medal at the Atlanta Olympics
"The Sixth Sense," starringBruce Willis, Toni Collette, andHaley Joel Osment, premieres
Breaking Dawn, the fourth book inStephenie Meyer's Twilight Saga, is published by Little, Brown at midnight with a print run of 3.7 million copies
Michael Phelpsends the World Swimming Championships in Rome with his fifth gold medal as part of the US 4 x 100 m medley relay team, which records the 43rd world record of the controversial meet
Carl Icahnsues computer giant Dell in an attempt to derail a buyout bid by the CEO,Michael Dell
Great Britain'sPrince Philip, aged 96, makes his final solo public appearance before retiring from public engagements
US PresidentDonald Trumpsigns legislation imposing sanctions on Russia, limiting his ability to ever lift them
Singer-songwriterEd Sheeran's Divide tour becomes the most attended and highest-grossing tour of all time, overtaking U2 after 893 days on the road
SpaceXDragon capsule carrying NASA astronauts Doug Hurley and Bob Behnken splashes down in the Gulf of Mexico, completing the first commercial crewed mission
70% of American have had at least one COVID-19 vaccination shot, a target PresidentJoe Bidenhad hoped to meet by 4 July
US House SpeakerNancy Pelosiis the first speaker to visit Taiwan in 25 years, prompting condemnation from the Chinese Communist Party
American rock band Aerosmith cancels their "Peace Out: The Farewell Tour" and retires from touring due to a vocal cord injury of singerSteven Tyler(76)
Formal signing of the US Declaration of Independence by 56 people (date most accepted by modern historians)

After 3½ days suffering exhaustion, lack of water, and shark attacks in the Philippine Sea, the surviving crew of USS Indianapolis are spotted by Wilbur “Chuck” Gwinn, a PV-1 Ventura pilot on a routine sector search; 316 survive
Potsdam Conference betweenJoseph Stalin, Harry Truman, and Winston Churchill (replaced by Clement Attlee after losing the 1945 general election) ends
NASA confirms Voyager 2's discovery of three more moons of Neptune, designated temporarily as 1989 N2, 1989 N3, and 1989 N24
Johan Cicero, Elector of Brandenburg (1486-99), born in Ansbach (d. 1499)
Theodor Zwinger, Swiss physician and scholar, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1588)
Boris Godunov, Tsar of Russia (1598-1605) and chief adviser to Tsar Fyodor I, born in Vyazma, Russia (d. 1605)
Samuel Dirksz van Hoogstraten, Dutch painter, born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1678)
Kaspar von Stieler, German poet (Teutsche Wolredner), born in Erfurt, Germany (d. 1707)
Johann Jakob Scheuchzer, Swiss scholar, born in Zürich, Switzerland (d. 1733)
Philippe II, Duke of Orléans and Regent of France (1715-23), born in Château de Saint Cloud, France (d. 1723)
Mahmud I, Sultan of Ottoman Empire (1730-54) who fought Austria & Russia, born in Edirne Palace, Edirne, Ottoman Empire (d. 1754)
Lorenzo Ricci, Italian Jesuit leader (18th & last Superior General of the Society of Jesus), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1775)

John Kieran, American columnist and author (Natural History of NYC), born in New York City (d. 1981)

Hal Mohr [Harold Leon Mohr], American Cinematographer and husband of Evelyn Venable (Captain Blood), born in San Francisco, California (d. 1974)

Lorenzo Herrera, Venezuelan singer and composer, born in Caracas, Venezuela (d. 1960)

Roger MacDougall, Scottish playwright and screenwriter (The Man in the White Suit), born in Glasgow (d. 1993)
Truus van Aalten [Geertruida van Aalten], Dutch silent and sound screen actress (Flaming Youth; The Beggar Student; The Girl in the Blue Hat), born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1999)
